coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposites of nitrogen/zinc-doped carbon dots@ hydrotalcite with highly fluorescent in solid-state for visualization of latent fingerprints
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Da-Wu
Zhang, Xing-Fu
Zhang, Xiao-Tong
lv, Xiao-Sen
You, Nan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report on the optical performances of a newly developed nanocomposite based on N-doped carbon dots with Zn-assisted modification (Zn/N-CDs) using hydrotalcite as the support (Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite) for the enhanced solid-state fluorescence in its application of the visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs). The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can interact with universal components of the LFPs residues through electrostatic interactions and physical adsorption. Good dispersion of the Zn/N-CDs on the surface of hydrotalcite overcomes aggregation-induced quenching effect, and emits the bright green fluorescence of 525 nm under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light in solid states, providing a well-defined platform for the visualization of LFPs. The feasibility of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite as a fluorescent label for the visualization of LFPs is tested. The results illustrate that the well-defined details of ridge patterns in level 2 and level 3 of the LFPs characteristics on various substrates (such as glass, aluminum foil, tiles, plastic packing of beer bottle, paper and leather) are clearly identified for individual identification using conventional powder dusting method. Green emission of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can provide sufficient contrast to background interferences of substrate surfaces. The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite with high sensitivity and wide applicability has great potential as a promising alternative for the visualization of LFPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano-sized
Carbon dots
Hydrotalcite
Latent fingerprints
Detection

Ministry of Public Security of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Foundation of Basic Special Project by the Ministry of Public Security of China (No: 2021JC07 ), Open Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of trace inspection and identification technology (No: 2021002 and HJ2022002KF ), Shanghai Key Laboratory of Crime Scene Evidence (No: 2016XCW2K03 ), the Key Laboratory of Impression Evidence Examination and Identification Technology (No: HJ2021002KF ), Ministry of Public Security of China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM, (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM images of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite.
(a) FT-IR and (b) Raman spectra of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite.
(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) high resolution C1s spectra, (c) high resolution N1s spectra and (d) XRD patterns of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) excitation and emission spectra of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite.

Fluorescent image of the LFPs developed by the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite (under 365 nm UV light with yellow filter) showing primary, secondary-minutiae [1] Scar [2], core [3], Double bifurcation [4], island [5] bifurcation [6], termination and tertiary level details [7]: Sweat pores and [8] ridge and furrow.
Fluorescent images of LFPs on different substrate surfaces under 365 nm UV light with yellow filter: (a) aluminum foil (b) tiles, (c) plastic packing of beer bottle, (d) paper, and (e) leather.
Fluorescent images of LFPs aged for different times on glass slide under 365 nm UV light with yellow filter (a:1 day; b: 5 days, c: 9 days, d: 15 days and e: 21 days).

head.ce:title: Nanocomposites of nitrogen/zinc-doped carbon dots@ hydrotalcite with highly fluorescent in solid-state for visualization of latent fingerprints

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Forensic Science, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Criminal Investigation Police University of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report on the optical performances of a newly developed nanocomposite based on N-doped carbon dots with Zn-assisted modification (Zn/N-CDs) using hydrotalcite as the support (Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite) for the enhanced solid-state fluorescence in its application of the visualization of latent fingerprints (LFPs). The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can interact with universal components of the LFPs residues through electrostatic interactions and physical adsorption. Good dispersion of the Zn/N-CDs on the surface of hydrotalcite overcomes aggregation-induced quenching effect, and emits the bright green fluorescence of 525 nm under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light in solid states, providing a well-defined platform for the visualization of LFPs. The feasibility of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite as a fluorescent label for the visualization of LFPs is tested. The results illustrate that the well-defined details of ridge patterns in level 2 and level 3 of the LFPs characteristics on various substrates (such as glass, aluminum foil, tiles, plastic packing of beer bottle, paper and leather) are clearly identified for individual identification using conventional powder dusting method. Green emission of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can provide sufficient contrast to background interferences of substrate surfaces. The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite with high sensitivity and wide applicability has great potential as a promising alternative for the visualization of LFPs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprints as the unique signatures for individuals are one of most common traces in the crime scene [1]. Latent fingerprints (LFPs), an unseen impression of fingerprints left on the surface of the objects, can be detected by some physical and chemical techniques [2]. Therefore, the visualization of LFPs becomes the challenges to the forensic sciences.

Some traditional methods such as powder dusting, iodine fuming, fluorescent dyes, vacuum metal deposition, ninhydrin, 1,2-indanedione are used to visualize the LFPs [3–5]. Recently, adsorption by fluorescent nanoparticles has emerged for the detection of LFPs [6]. Some fluorescent reagents (such as quantum dots [7], rare earth oxide [8] and perovskite [9]) have been extensively employed in the detection of LFPs. However, the wide application of these fluorescent reagents is limited due to their high cost or/and high toxicity [10]. Carbon dots (CDs) with a diameter less than 10 nm exhibit an excellent fluorescent property and water solubility, and provide the high sensitivity, prominent contrast against the background and good selectivity. More importantly, CDs are easily functionalized with other groups to enhance the visualization of LFPs and has great potential in forensic application [11]. Numerous studies reports that the water-soluble CDs are employed as a liquid luminescent reagent for the detection of LFPs with the performances of good contrast and ridges details [12–14]. However, the practical performance of CDs is unsatisfactory. The CD solutions is unsuitable for the permeable substrates, while the background staining is also found because more of the CDs emit blue fluorescence which is easily interfered by the self-fluorescence from a back ground like paper. Continuous improvement of the luminescence characteristic of the CDs draws recently more attention. There are much more differences on the fluorescence features of CDs due to the used different source materials [12]. Therefore, a change in the color emissive CDs can be observed through using the different precursors (such as dl-maleic acid and ethylenediamine for green emissive CDs solution [13], Rhodamine B for orange emissive CDs solution [14], and phthalic acid and piperazine for yellowish emissive CDs solution [15]). In addition, Zinc (Zn), an important element assisting the electron-transfer process, is a promising metal dopant for CDs [16]. The Zn-CDs can increase the photoluminescence quantum yield and improve the luminescence characteristic, which are conducive to the visualization of LFPs [17,18]. Another challenge of CDs is severe fluorescence quenching in the solid-state due to the resonance energy transfer and direct π–π interactions. Some CDs based nanocomposites in the solid-state are developed by embedding CDs onto the solid-state supports such as SiO2 [19], metal-organic framework materials [20], ZnO [21], polymer [22], and mineral [23] to avoid the problem of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the selection of solid-state supports plays a key role in the preparation of the CDs based phosphors. Hydrotalcite as an advantage support is paid an attention due to its special sandwich, controllability of layers interval and anionic exchanging [24]. Whereas, there is no research on the fluorescent nanocomposites of CDs and hydrotalcite in the solid-state for the visualization of LFPs.

Herein, we report the nanocomposites by embedding the as-fabricated CDs nanoparticles onto the hydrotalcite as a phosphor for the visualization of LFPs. The zinc/nitrogen-doped CDs (Zn/N-CDs) are first synthesized via hydrothermal method using citric acid, ethylenediamine and ZnCl2 as the precursors, and then the Zn/N-CDs as crystallization agents are grown on the hydrotalcite by hydrothermal method for the preparation of the nanocomposites (Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite). The fluorescence images of LFPs on the different substrates using the nanocomposites of Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite by the powder-dust blowing method are obtained with 365 nm light irradiation.

All the chemicals used were of analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Shanghai, China. The products are characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence, Raman, FT-IR and UV–vis spectroscopy.

Zn/N-CDs are prepared according to the literature previously reported [24]. The preparation process is as follows: Citric acid (1.5 g), ethylenediamine (7.5 mL) and ZnCl2 (0.38 g) at the molar ratio of 2:2:1 are mixed and dissolved with 50 mL of deionized water. And then the mixture solution is adjusted the solution pH to 2 by adding 1 mol L−1 HCl solution, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 6 h by hydrothermal treatment. After the reaction, the brown-black solution is purified by dialysis process using a dialysis bag with retained molecular weight of 7000 Da MWCO. And then the resulting brown-yellow solution is freeze-dried for further use. Hydrotalcite (3 g) and Zn/N-CDs powder (1.5 g) is mixed for the preparation of the nanocomposites using the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C. After 3 h, the resultant precipitates are collected by filtration. The obtained powder is washed with deionized water for several times, and then dried at 110 °C for 3 h. The yellowish powder of the nanocomposites is obtained for further use.

All the LFPs are collected from a same person using a standard procedure as follows: The cleaned finger with soap gently wipes the forehead, and then the LFPs are pressed on the different surfaces of the nine substrates including a clear glass slide, aluminum foil, tiles, plastic packing of beer bottle, paper, and leather. All the deposited LFPs are aged for 24 h at ambient conditions before the visualization. Powder dusting method is employed to visualize the LFPs. The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@ hydrotalcite is ground to fine powder and then sieved with 100 mesh prior to dusting process. The powder of nanocomposites is carefully introduced onto the substrate with the LFPs and the excess is removed by blow powder method. The images of LFPs using 365 nm light radiation are recorded with a digital camera (Canon EOS 50OD). Scheme S1 displays the general process for the identification of the LFPs based on the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@ hydrotalcite. For identifying the LFPs aged, the LFPs are left on several clean glass slides which are placed in a sealed and clean plastic box to prevent contamination and stored for 1, 5, 9, 15 and 21 days, respectively. The visualization of the LFPs aged on glass slides is performed by powder dusting method. All the process of the LFPs visualization is carried out at room temperature.

The surface morphology of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite is tested by SEM at the 1 μm scale bar and TEM at the 100 nm scale bar in Fig. 1 . The Zn/N-CDs particles are deposited on the surface of hydrotalcite (Fig. 1a), illustrating that the Zn/N-CDs particles have the good dispersion on the hydrotalcite. A representative TEM image of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite is indicated in Fig. 1b. Most of the Zn/N-CDs nanoparticles are near spherical shape in morphology. The size distribution of the Zn/N-CDs nanoparticles can be seen to be relatively small with an average size of ∼5 nm and is consistent with previously described results [25]. The image of high-resolution TEM (in Fig. 1c) reveals the lattice spacing as 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (100) fact of graphitic structure [26,27].

The FT-IR spectrum of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite indicated in Fig. 2 a, displays several prominent peaks. A broad and intense band at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to the O–H stretching mode, indicating the existence of large numbers of hydroxyl groups. The bands at 2920 and 2850 cm−1 correspond to the C–H asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of methylene groups. The peaks at 1402, 1455 and 1512 cm−1 correspond to the CC stretching mode of the aromatic hydrocarbons [27]. The peaks in the range of 1000–1300 cm−1 are due to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C [28]. The bands in low frequency region (663, 552, and 448 cm−1) are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of M − O and O–M–O (M = Mg, Al) [29], indicating that the presence of hydrotalcite in the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite. As seen in Fig. 2b, Raman spectrum of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite indicates two characteristic bands which are interpreted as the D band at 1330 cm−1 from the defects and disorders in sp3-hybridized carbon system and the G band at 1587 cm−1 from the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon in graphite crystals [30].

The chemical structure of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite is further explored by XPS measurements. The XPS spectrum demonstrates four the predominant peaks at 286, 398, 530 and 1020 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s (Fig. 3 a), N1s (Fig. 3b), O1s (Fig. S1) and Zn2p (Fig. 3c), respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s can be fitted into four peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.8 and 288.9 eV, which correspond to the C–C, C–N, C–O and CO bonds, respectively [31]. The results are consistent with the FT-IR spectroscopic analysis above, indicating that the as-fabricated Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite are equipped with –COOH and –OH groups. In the N1s spectra, two peaks located at 397.9 and 404.4 eV indicate the presence of amino N and NH/NH2, respectively [26,32]. XRD of the as-fabricated Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite (Fig. 3d) reveals the characteristic diffraction peaks of the hydrotalcite are located at 11, 24, 34, 39, 60 and 62° due to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110) and (113) crystallographic planes [33]. The diffraction peak around 23° is due to the (002) planes of the graphite structure [34].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite in solid state reveals two peaks at around 260 and 340 nm (Fig. 4 a), which are due to the π- π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [35]. The strong solid-state fluorescence is displayed due to good dispersion of the Zn/N-CDs on the hydrophilic surface of hydrotalcite (Fig. 4b). The excitation spectrum of the Zn/N-CDs@ hydrotalcite in solid state exhibits two peaks centered at 345 nm and 253 nm, which are attributed to the trapping of excited surface states [36], while, the emission spectrum of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite in solid state presents a wide peak centered at 525 nm, leading to that the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite in solid state emits strong green fluorescence. Interestingly, there is no significant difference in the excitation wavelengths with excitation at 345 and 253 nm, which is due to the homogeneous surface of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite [37]. The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite is the yellowish powder in daylight, and emits green fluorescence under irradiation with a 365 nm UV-light lamp (Fig. S2).

Zn2+ ions can enhance the intensity of emission with small blue shift of λmax as reported previously [26,38]. Compared with the nanocomposites without Zn2+, the emission of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite with the fluorescence intensity enhanced is due to the coordination induced surface passivation of carbon dots and modulation of surface energy traps [16,26]. In addition, the UV–vis spectra of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite indicates a broad spectrum with blue shift of maximal absorption peak from 380 nm to 345 nm (Fig. S3a), which agree well with the literature reported [26]. While the fluorescence spectra of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite happen a blue shift of strong emission peak from 540 nm to 525 nm with respective to the nanocomposites without Zn2+ (Fig. S3b). Therefore, Zn2+ ions play an important role in improvement of the optical properties of the CDs-based nanocomposites.

The Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite with strong fluorescence in solid-state as the phosphor for the visualization of LFPs is explored by conventional powder dusting method. The LFPs are first deposited on clean a glass slide, and then developed with the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite. The components (such as moisture, basic proteins or/and oily) of the residue LFPs can interact with functional groups (such as amine and carboxylic) on the surface of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite through physical adsorption and electrostatic attraction, which is consistent with the visualization mechanism for the LFPs by conventional powder method [39,40]. The LFPs image visualized by the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite emits the bright green fluorescence at 525 nm under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, which is profit to visualize the LFPs without the use of the specialized excitation source. No fluorescence is observed in the places uncovered by the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite, thereby enhancing the contrast for the visualization of LFPs. However, auto-fluorescent background is usually observed under 365 nm UV light [41]. Green emission of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can assists the distinction of LFPs from the auto-fluorescent background with the clear exhibition of the ridge patterns of the LFPs [36]. As shown in Fig. 5 , there is almost no background interference of blue fluorescence on glass slide substrate due to the green emission of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite with sufficient contrast to the blue auto-fluorescent background [36]. Analysis of the image clearly reveals most of the informative properties of the LFPs which can be categorized into three levels for the fingerprint individuality. Level 1 information of ridge patterns such as arches, loops, and whorls can be easily identified (Fig. 5). Identification of more detailed characteristics at the level 2 and 3 can provide a basis for the high-resolution recognition of the LFPs. Clearly visible details about minutia points of level 2 such as scar, core, island, bifurcation, and termination, tertiary level details can be visualized (Fig. 5). Furthermore, level 3 details about sweat pores are also found and the amplitudes of LFPs vary significantly from ridge to furrow (Fig. 5). The information obtained play a vital role in forensic analysis and are propitious for crime scene investigation to identify the individual. The results indicate that the LFPs features with the high-resolution based on the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite are feasible.

To explore the applicability of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite, several typical substrates (such as glass slide, aluminum foil, tiles, plastic packing of beer bottle, paper, and leather) are chosen for depositing the LFPs. The background interferences of the surfaces coloured or surfaces with auto-fluorescent on the visualization of the LFPs are tested. The LFPs images visualized on various substrates clearly identify the details of ridge patterns which can bestow information for individual identification (Fig. 6 ). The as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite as the phosphor illustrates the good sensitivity and reliability for individual identification of the LFPs. Moreover, there are no significant background interferences of the substrates tested on the visualization of the LFPs. The green strong emission of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can present high contrast to the background, and overcome the reflection light from substrates. These results suggest that the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can serve for the recognition of the LFPs on a variety of real substrates with high-quality and high-contrast without notable background interferences.

In real crime scene investigations, the LFPs found are not often fresh and have been left for a long time. The LFPs aged are usually difficult to detect due to the change in composition [42]. In order to extend the applicability of the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite, the visualization of the LFPs aged is further investigated on glass slide. The LFPs aged for up to 21 days are visualized by powder dusting method. Most of characteristics of the LFPs aged left on glass slide with high resolution and high contrast are displayed (Fig. 7 ). The LFPs aged with different time has little negative influence on the exhibition of the LFPs characteristics even those aged 15 days. After 21 days, the unclear details about ridge patterns of the LFPs is observed due to the loss of the LFPs residue on the glass slide. These images with details can be used to confirm individual identification, suggesting that the as-prepared Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite could be used to identify the LFPs left from the past.

The N-doped carbon dots with Zn-assisted modification are dispersed well on the surface of hydrotalcite for preventing the aggregation-induced quenching effect, resulting in the enhancement of solid-state luminescence properties of the nanocomposite. Highly stable green fluorescence of the Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite can clearly identify the level 2 and level 3 ridge details of the fresh and aged LFPs on glass slide. Various porous and non-porous surfaces of substrates have no significant background interferences on the LFPs visualization for individual identification. The Zn/N-CDs@hydrotalcite as a highly efficient phosphor have great potential for the detection of LFPs in forensic science.

Da-Wu Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation, Writing – review & editing. Xing-Fu Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao-Tong Zhang: Supervision, Validation. Xiao-Sen lv: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources. Nan You: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Foundation of Basic Special Project by the Ministry of Public Security of China (No: 2021JC07), Open Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of trace inspection and identification technology (No: 2021002 and HJ2022002KF), Shanghai Key Laboratory of Crime Scene Evidence (No: 2016XCW2K03), the Key Laboratory of Impression Evidence Examination and Identification Technology (No: HJ2021002KF), Ministry of Public Security of China.
